content.problemStatement:
You are playing a game called the Reduction Game. This game is played on an array aaa with nnn integers. You also have another integer kkk with you. The following operation will be made repeatedly on this array:


Choose any two elements ai,aja_i, a_jai​,aj​ such that i≠ji \neq ji=j, and min(ai,aj)>kmin(a_i, a_j) > kmin(ai​,aj​)>k. Decrease both aia_iai​ and aja_jaj​ by one.
This operation should be applied to the array if it's possible to apply it. But if there are multiple valid choices of aia_iai​ and aja_jaj​, you can choose which ones to do first and so on. The aim is to apply the operations in such a way, so as to maximize the sum of the elements in the array at the end. Your task is to find the maximum possible sum of the final array.

###Input


The first line of the input contains an integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of the test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains two integers n,kn, kn,k denoting the number of elements in the array aaa.
The second line contains nnn space-separated integers denoting the array aaa.
###Output:
For each test case, output an integer corresponding to the maximum possible sum of the remaining array in a new line.

###Constraints


1≤T≤50001 \leq T \leq 50001≤T≤5000
1≤n≤501 \leq n \leq 501≤n≤50
1≤ai,k≤500001 \leq a_i, k \leq 500001≤ai​,k≤50000
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines. The first line contains two integers n and k, where n is the number of elements in the array and k is the given integer. The second line contains n space-separated integers denoting the array.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output the maximum possible sum of the final array in a new line.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Explanation: The initial array is [1, 2]. In this case, there are no two elements > 1. So, no operations can be made, and this is the only final array possible. And the sum of this is 3, and hence that is the answer.

Explanation: The initial array is [3, 2, 2]. We can decrease the first and the last elements by one to get [2, 2, 1]. Then we can decrease the first and the second elements by one to get [1, 1, 1]. The sum of this array is 3.

Explanation: The initial array is [4, 5]. We can decrease the first and the second elements by one to get [3, 4]. Then we can decrease the first and the second elements by one to get [2, 3]. The sum of this array is 5.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use a greedy approach. We will repeatedly apply the operation to the array until no more operations are possible. In each step, we will choose the two elements with the minimum values that are greater than k and decrease them by one. This approach ensures that we maximize the sum of the elements in the array at the end.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * n^2) — where T is the number of test cases and n is the number of elements in the array. The reason is that in the worst case, we might need to apply the operation n times for each test case, and each operation takes O(n) time to find the two elements with the minimum values.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(n) — where n is the number of elements in the array. The reason is that we need to store the array in memory.

code.Python3:
import sys
input = sys.stdin.readline
for _ in range(int(input())):
    n, k = map(int, input().split())
    arr = list(map(int, input().split()))
    while True:
        min1 = -1
        min2 = -1
        for i in range(n):
            if arr[i] > k and (min1 == -1 or arr[i] < arr[min1]):
                min2 = min1
                min1 = i
            elif arr[i] > k and (min2 == -1 or arr[i] < arr[min2]):
                min2 = i
        if min1 == -1 or min2 == -1: break
        arr[min1] -= 1
        arr[min2] -= 1
    print(sum(arr))
